chapters.title: Scaling up boosts productivity in any company
chapters.text:
For most entrepreneurs, growing a business feels like a leap they would rather not take. Scaling up means change, and change pulls people away from the routines they already know. That is why so few companies manage to pull it off. A few common barriers show up again and again. Leadership can hold a company back when there are not enough leaders willing to delegate. Infrastructure becomes a problem when the systems and resources cannot keep up with the decisions that come with growth. Marketing turns into a hurdle when the company cannot build a real function that pulls in new buyers.

To clear these hurdles, a company has to set rules that everyone follows, and those rules should become the company's core values. Next comes a strategy that puts the customer first and sets the business apart from the competition. Breaking big jobs into smaller pieces using a WWW approach, which stands for Who, What, When, makes execution easier and helps with management, communication, and accountability. Cash management is the final piece, and it keeps the plan moving on schedule.

Scaling up is not easy, and problems will pop up along the way. A solid strategy is the best way to deal with those problems early and to put the business in a stronger place.

If a company is making less profit than it should, it is time to think about scaling up.

This summary will break down what it means to scale up a business. It will also show how core values and clear boundaries help a company grow.

Letting go of control and trusting others to do good work is one of the hardest parts of leading a growing organization.

chapters.title: A company needs the right people on the bus
chapters.text:
The people around a leader matter when it is time to scale up. People inside the company and outside it, including investors, customers, and suppliers, all play a part in the outcome. There are two questions that every business leader should ask if they want their company to grow. First, am I happy, and are there partners or investors making my life harder than it needs to be? Second, would I enthusiastically rehire every person on my team, knowing what I know today?

Answering these questions honestly can save a leader a lot of emotional drain. Business owners should pay close attention to how they attract and hire their staff. Anyone can put a person in a seat, but not everyone can put the right person there. Leaders cannot afford to copy what the competition does, so the hiring process and the strategy behind it have to be their own. Hire people who share the company's values and who believe in them.

Clear values and a clear purpose help a company pull in the right people.

Sometimes business owners have to put extra energy into finding the right hires, because the larger a company gets, the more careful it has to be about who comes on board. A company that does not split its team into the right roles, that does not give authority where it is due, and that does not hold leaders accountable will eventually fall apart. It is a lot like a cell. A cell grows until it has to divide, and if it tries to keep growing without dividing, it cannot take in enough nutrients or get rid of waste, and it dies from within.

chapters.title: Good management keeps the team happy and engaged
chapters.text:
One of the biggest obstacles to growth is the lack of strong managerial leadership. Managers in a growing company have five main jobs to do. They hire fewer people but pay them well. They recognize people and show appreciation. They set clear expectations and give each employee a clear line of sight. They stop demotivating and start dehassling, which means clearing the unnecessary friction. They help people play to their strengths.

Hiring fewer people and paying them more is a smart way to start. Hire one strong person instead of three, train them well, and pay them more than the average competitor. That step alone can do a lot for the company's growth.

Recognition and appreciation mean a lot to people, especially when it comes from a manager. Feeling seen helps a company keep its best performers. It is one habit that every manager should build into the workweek.

Great managers care about the differences between their team members and how those differences affect the work.

Clear expectations and a clear line of sight help every team member see where the company is going and how their work fits in. Managers should make sure that the daily, weekly, and quarterly targets line up with the bigger picture.

Stop demotivating and start dehassling. Every manager has to keep an eye on what kills motivation, knowing that the right answer changes from team to team. That may mean letting someone go, hiring a new face, or cutting processes that slow people down. The goal is the same, which is to give the team the best working environment possible.

Help people play to their strengths. A good manager knows what gives each person energy and what drains them, and they hand out work based on that knowledge. A great manager can describe each team member's strengths, personality, and recent wins in detail.

chapters.title: A strong core is the foundation of good strategy
chapters.text:
Scaling up depends on having a clear, original strategy that delivers on the company's promises. Core values are the rules and boundaries that shape a company's culture and personality, and they guide the behavior and decisions of everyone who works there. Managers have to lead by example, making sure that how they live lines up with the company's values. When the values are clear, the company runs smoothly, and employees know the right thing to do when a hard call comes their way.

Core values, purpose, competencies, and strategy are the pieces that build a vision worth scaling up.

Core competencies make the strategy sharper and make the company easier to work with. The core purpose sits at the heart of the company. It is why an employee chooses to work there, and it is why a customer chooses to buy. The purpose should come from a sincere place and should reach beyond just making money. The core competencies set the edges of what a company does well, and they help managers and employees understand how to use their skills.

If the core values are the soul of the organization, the core purpose gives it heart.

When it comes to strategy planning, having everyone on the same page makes everything easier. Everyone should know what strategy the company is following, and the strategy itself should be as simple as possible.

chapters.title: A great idea still has to be executed
chapters.text:
Putting a strategy into action takes more than a good plan. Every business leader should pay attention to three things, including priorities, data, and meeting rhythms.

Priorities are the first piece. Business managers should pick the most important, measurable focus for each quarter. A company with too many priorities often ends up achieving none of them. Sticking to a single priority per quarter, for example one big goal at a time, makes the company stronger and gives everyone a clear target to support.

A healthy team is one where every voice is heard and considered.

Data is the second piece. Effective execution depends on gathering data from stakeholders and customers. Speed matters here, and gathering data on a regular schedule, for as long as possible, makes the process easier. The company uses the data as feedback to guide decisions. Management can use that feedback to bring employees into the conversation about specific products and choices. This loop tightens the link between employees and management. Gathering the data is only part of the work, though. Analyzing it, collecting feedback, and understanding how the data affects customers and products is what closes the loop.

Meeting rhythm is the third piece. After setting priorities and gathering data, the next step is to build a steady meeting rhythm. Regular meetings, including daily, monthly, quarterly, and annual gatherings, help the company handle communication and reach its goals. They also give the team a chance to talk through the most important issues facing the business.

To get good at execution, leaders have to listen to their customers and employees, gather data, hold regular meetings to discuss the data, and set priorities based on what they find.

Goals without routines are just wishes, and routines without goals wander in circles. The best business leaders have a clear vision and the disciplines, or routines, to make it real.

chapters.title: A thriving business needs healthy cash flow
chapters.text:
Scaling up costs real money. At this stage, business owners need to find ways to bring in profit and cash flow on their own, instead of leaning on loans. That kind of growth takes careful work and a clear sense of the company's cash conversion cycle, which is the time it takes for the company to spend cash and then take that same cash back in. The shorter the cycle, the more money the company makes. To run the cycle well, the company has to know how much it earns each day, why some customers pay late, and how to set up the right rewards for the team.

Speeding up every part of the cycle helps the cash come in faster. When products ship quickly, customers can buy sooner. Cutting mistakes out of the process makes customers more willing to pay on time, because nobody likes dealing with errors.

Every step in the right direction adds up to the company's core success.

Cash is only part of the picture. The accounting process matters just as much. Good accounting helps the company make smarter financial decisions and spot trends. Accounting is not only for tax time. It tracks cash flow, builds the financial data the company needs, and helps with forecasting and debt management. For example, Harnish told the story of a company that hired Greg Crabtree's accounting team after losing money for 14 years, even with five million dollars in revenue. Within five years, the company had grown to twenty-five million in revenue, the owner was debt free, and the business was generating real after-tax income.

According to the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development, around 57 percent of leaders have been through a moment when they feared their business might fail.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
In scaling up a company, everything counts, from the kind of people the company hires to the strategy it picks and the way it manages cash. Everyone in the company has to know the core values and the role they play in the long game. Leaders have to bring in the right people, people who are excited about scaling up.

Managers need to be plugged into what is happening at every stage of growth. That does not mean doing all the work themselves, but it does mean staying aware. If the company is growing, remember that cash flow is just as important as profit. That is how companies like Amazon keep growing even when they report a loss. In 2013, Amazon's business model brought in around three billion dollars in cash flow, which fueled real growth even in a year of reported losses.

Management should make sure every quarter has a clear goal and that the team is moving toward it. A weekly leadership council can set the company's priorities and pick a theme for the quarter. Routines are a great way to set a standard for both leaders and employees, because they keep everyone aligned on the company's goals and the strategies that will get them there. No single playbook fits every company, but it is always possible to try today, try again tomorrow, and take it one step at a time.

Try this

Build a monthly, weekly, and daily routine of touchpoints for the management team and the rest of the staff to talk through how to grow the company. For example, hold a five to fifteen minute meeting each day to cover the most important issues and quick updates, and move anything bigger to the weekly meeting.

Did you make it to the end?
